interpretation:
Discrete monitoring biases a knock-out upward, and by far more than intuition allows. The barrier is unobserved between fixes, so excursions that would have killed the option go unseen and it survives paths it should not -- making it worth more than the continuously monitored contract the closed form prices. Every one of the 42 resolved cells is biased high, across both barrier directions.

The size is the finding, and it is worst exactly where the contract is cheapest. An up-and-out call struck at the spot with its barrier 5% above knocks out in precisely the states where it would have paid, so what survives is worth almost nothing -- 0.0088 here -- and daily monitoring biases that by +67% of its own value. A down-and-out 0.26 sigma*sqrt(T) away is biased +10.1% at the same frequency; further out, +3.0% and +0.28%. The bias tracks sigma*sqrt(dt), the distance the price can wander unobserved: at daily monitoring a down-and-out at B=90 runs +0.30%, +3.01%, +10.03% of price at volatilities of 0.1, 0.2 and 0.4. None of this is about the calendar.

The decay rate needs care, and this experiment is a good illustration of why a fitted slope is not a measured order. Theory (Broadie-Glasserman-Kou) says the bias decays as O(1/sqrt(m)) -- order 0.5 against the monitoring interval. Almost no fitted interval here contains 0.5, and, revealingly, they miss it from *both sides*: down-barriers come in low (0.248, 0.395, 0.437 as the barrier nears the spot) while close up-barriers come in high (0.630 at B=105, 0.552 at B=110). A single-direction study would have read its own miss as a signed correction to theory; seeing both signs shows it for what it is -- the higher-order terms the asymptotic statement discards, which carry opposite signs for the two geometries. The local orders climb monotonically toward 0.5 for down-barriers (0.342, 0.375, 0.390, 0.431, 0.435 at B=90) and the asymptotic-window fits recover 0.5 at five of the seven fitted arms. The measured statement is that the bias decays at a rate approaching 0.5 over the frequencies tested, not that it decays at 0.5, and not that theory is wrong.

What settles that is a check needing no fit: the same theory predicts the bias's *size* in closed form, by moving the barrier away from the spot through the mean overshoot exp(+/- beta*sigma*sqrt(dt)). It was not fitted to this data -- beta is -zeta(1/2)/sqrt(2*pi) -- and for down-barriers it lands within 2 across-seed standard errors at 16 of 18 resolved cells. A theory with the rate wrong could not predict the size to within its noise across three barriers and six frequencies.

For up-barriers the same correction is resolved as *wrong* at 23 of the 24 cells, and that is the most informative result here. It is not a sign error: the shift direction was checked independently, and reversing it misses by an order of magnitude rather than a few percent. The residual is systematically negative -- the correction over-predicts -- and small in absolute terms (0.0006 to 0.008), running -275 standard errors at B=105, m=5 and draining to -5.4 by m=250. The single cell that does *not* disagree is the farthest up-barrier at the finest monitoring, B=140 at m=250 -- exactly where an o(1/sqrt(m)) remainder should have drained away, which is the behaviour the theory predicts rather than an exception to it. Two things make it visible. First, an up-and-out call near the spot pays on almost no paths, so the estimator's variance collapses and a remainder that would hide under a down-barrier's noise is glaring here: the same absolute error is 0.1% of a down-barrier price and 4% of this one. Second, the geometry is genuinely harder. An up-and-out call pays B-K *just below* its barrier and zero at it, so the value is discontinuous across the boundary -- while a down-and-out call struck above its barrier approaches zero smoothly there. A continuity correction built on an overshoot argument is weakest exactly where the value jumps. So the experiment does not merely use the correction as an oracle; it locates where the oracle stops being trustworthy, and the answer is not where a down-barrier-only study would have suggested.

The Brownian-bridge correction removes the bias rather than shrinking it, in both directions. Between two observed points log-GBM is a Brownian bridge whose crossing probability is known exactly, so a simulation can account for the excursions it did not observe instead of pretending they did not happen -- and because log-GBM is exactly Brownian motion with drift, that correction is exact rather than asymptotic. No bridge cell shows a bias this run can resolve: the largest across all 48 is 2.4 across-seed standard errors, against a discrete arm reaching 563. It holds at m=5 as well as m=250, which is the point -- it is a correction, not a refinement, and five observations with the bridge beat 250 without it.

The PDE arm answers a different question and anchors the whole record. It prices the *continuous* contract directly -- solving the Black-Scholes equation with an absorbing boundary at the barrier -- rather than measuring a monitoring bias, and it converges to the same analytic reference the discrete arm measures against, at the second order Crank-Nicolson with Rannacher smoothing predicts. Two independent routes therefore reach one continuous price: the closed form the analytic engine evaluates, and the grid the PDE refines toward it. The Monte Carlo bias and the PDE's grid error are then errors against the same number, which is what lets the two arms sit in one experiment without conflating the discretisation of a path with the discretisation of an observation. The fitted orders are in `pde_convergence`; the arm fails the record if any falls below a second-order floor, since a misplaced barrier boundary would show up there first.

The practical reading is that the monitoring convention is a contract term worth more than most modelling choices, and that its cost is not uniform: it concentrates in the cheap, nearly-knocked-out contracts where a relative error is largest and a trader's intuition is weakest. It is a term of the contract, not an approximation to be tuned away, and a system that leaves it implicit will silently price one contract and report another.
limitations: The continuity correction is resolved as wrong at 23 of 24 resolved up_and_out cells, so it is not an oracle in that geometry. It is kept in the record as a measured reference that fails here, not removed, because where an approximation breaks down is itself a result.

The path is simulated with the exact scheme at exactly the monitoring dates, so it carries no path-discretisation error: log-GBM is Brownian motion with drift, and the exact scheme samples the true joint law of the price at any finite set of dates. What is measured here is therefore monitoring bias alone. A production simulation on a finer path grid, or with Euler steps, would additionally carry a path error this experiment cannot see and does not bound.

The Brownian-bridge correction is exact for geometric Brownian motion and for that reason only. Under Heston, or any model whose log-price is not Brownian motion with constant coefficients, the bridge law is an approximation, and nothing measured here says how good an approximation it is.
Calls only. Puts are a separate set of cases the analytic engine does not implement, so no trusted continuous reference exists for them.

Every bias reported here is measured against Reiner-Rubinstein, which is a model output rather than a market observation. It agrees with mpmath to 1e-15 and QuantLib to 1e-9, so it is a trustworthy statement about the model -- but this experiment measures agreement with a formula, not with reality.

At B=70, 1.78 sigma*sqrt(T) below the spot, the bias never clears 2.1 across-seed standard errors at any monitoring frequency. This run measured nothing there, and no order is reported for it. The bias is real -- theory says so, and the sign is right at four of six frequencies -- but it is smaller than 200000 paths across 16 seeds can see. Read as evidence that a distant barrier's monitoring bias is negligible at these path counts, not as evidence that it is zero.

The continuity correction is trustworthy for the down-barriers measured here and is NOT for the up-barriers: its residual is resolved at 23 of the 24 up-barrier cells, systematically negative, and still -5.4 standard errors at daily monitoring on the nearest one. The shift direction was verified independently -- reversing it misses by an order of magnitude rather than a few percent -- so this is the correction's own o(1/sqrt(m)) remainder, not a defect here. Do not use the continuity-corrected reference as an oracle for an up-and-out call near the spot; use the bridge, which is exact for this model.

The fitted orders that miss 0.5 are a property of the frequencies tested, not a measured contradiction of theory, and the record should not be quoted as though the two were the same. The evidence for that reading is circumstantial in the fits themselves -- climbing local orders, and a B=95 asymptotic window that contains 0.5 -- and direct only in the continuity-corrected check. Reaching the asymptotic range would need monitoring frequencies well beyond daily, which no traded contract uses and which this experiment therefore did not prioritise.

Read `continuity_corrected_residual_over_se`, not `bias_explained_fraction`, when judging the continuity correction. The fraction divides by the bias, so where the bias is small it is a noisy statistic and misleads: at B=80 it ranges from 67% to 109% across frequencies, which reads as the correction failing, while every residual there is within 1.9 standard errors of zero and therefore consistent with it being exactly right. The fraction is published because a reader will want it, not because it decides anything. Note also that a residual's significance depends on the estimator's noise, which varies by orders of magnitude across this grid: an up-and-out call near the spot pays on almost no paths, so its standard error collapses and the same absolute error that hides at B=70 is resolved at hundreds of sigma at B=105. Significance measures what this run can see, not how large the error is.

The 48 bridge cells share the same 16 seeds, so they are not 24 independent tests of the correction. Their sampling errors are correlated across barriers and frequencies -- visibly so: the largest positive deviation sits at m=50 for three of the four barriers. The evidence that the bridge is unbiased is weaker than the cell count suggests, though no individual cell comes close to the threshold.

The nearest barriers tested are 0.24-0.26 sigma*sqrt(T) from the spot. Barriers very close to the spot knock out nearly every path, leaving a small surviving sample and an estimator whose variance is dominated by rare paths. That regime is not characterised here.

The bridge estimator draws a Bernoulli knock decision per interval rather than accumulating the conditional survival probability. Both are unbiased, but the conditional-expectation form would have strictly lower variance. The bridge arm's intervals are wider than they need to be as a consequence of that choice, not of the correction itself.
name: Barrier Monitoring Bias
question: How much error results from discrete barrier monitoring?

arms.fit_refused: the bias cleared 3 standard errors at only 0 of 6 monitoring frequencies, so there is no measured decay to fit. A power law fitted to unresolved levels would report an order and an interval for what is sampling noise. The barrier is 1.78 sigma*sqrt(T) from the spot, far enough that discrete monitoring misses almost nothing: the bias is real but smaller than this run can see.
